name: Espresso
description: The foundation of Italian coffee culture, brewed by forcing near-boiling water through finely-ground coffee under 9 bars of pressure into a 30 ml shot. Delivers intense concentration crowned with a thick, hazel-coloured crema. Every milk-based espresso drink starts here.

name: Doppio
description: Italian for 'double' — two espresso shots pulled through a double-basket portafilter into a single demitasse cup. The standard order at most Italian bars, it offers twice the caffeine and a deeper, more layered flavour than a single shot. The building block of large milk-based drinks.

name: Ristretto
description: Italian for 'restricted' — the same dose of ground coffee as a standard espresso but extracted with only half the water (15–20 ml). The shortened extraction leaves behind bitter compounds, yielding a sweeter, syrupy, concentrated shot that many baristas prefer as the base for milk drinks.

name: Lungo
description: Italian for 'long' — espresso extracted with significantly more water (60–120 ml) over a longer pull time. This draws out more caffeine and bitter compounds, producing a lighter yet more bitter drink than a standard espresso. Not to be confused with an Americano, which adds water after brewing.

name: Americano
description: Hot water poured over one or two espresso shots to approximate the strength and volume of drip coffee. Legend says American soldiers in WWII-era Italy diluted espresso to make it feel like home. The result is a light-bodied, clean black coffee with an unmistakable espresso character underneath.

name: Cappuccino
description: One of Italy's most iconic exports: equal thirds of espresso, steamed milk, and thick microfoam served in a 150–180 ml cup. The velvety foam insulates heat and softens the espresso's edge. Traditionally a morning drink in Italy — locals never order it after 11 am.

name: Caffè Latte
description: A milkier cousin of the cappuccino: one or two espresso shots topped with 150–240 ml of steamed milk and a thin layer of microfoam. The high milk-to-espresso ratio makes it one of the most approachable espresso drinks and the perfect canvas for latte art.

name: Flat White
description: Born in Australia and New Zealand in the 1980s, a flat white packs a double ristretto into a smaller 150–160 ml cup with silky, barely-there microfoam. Stronger and more coffee-forward than a latte, without the thick foam of a cappuccino. A barista benchmark drink.

name: Espresso Macchiato
description: Italian for 'stained' — a straight espresso shot with just a dollop of foamed milk to soften its edge. At only 40–60 ml it is one of the smallest coffee drinks, prized by espresso purists who want a hint of milk without diluting the shot's intensity.

name: Cortado
description: Spanish for 'cut' — espresso cut with an equal part of warm milk to reduce acidity without adding foam. Served in a small 100–120 ml glass, the cortado sits between a macchiato and a flat white in both size and milk ratio. A staple of Spanish and Latin American café culture.

name: Caffè Mocha
description: A rich marriage of espresso, steamed milk, and chocolate syrup or cocoa powder, finished with whipped cream. Named after the Yemeni port city of Mocha, historically synonymous with naturally chocolatey coffee. Indulgent, sweet, and deeply satisfying — coffee meets dessert.

name: Affogato
description: Italian for 'drowned' — a scoop of vanilla gelato flooded with one or two hot espresso shots poured tableside. As the espresso melts the gelato, a magical bittersweet sauce forms. Served as dessert in Italy, it straddles the line between coffee drink and pudding.

name: Caffè Breve
description: An American variation on the latte that swaps steamed whole milk for steamed half-and-half (equal parts milk and cream). The result is extraordinarily rich and indulgent, with a denser, longer-lasting foam that coats the palate. Significantly higher in calories than a traditional latte.

name: Piccolo Latte
description: Italian for 'small' — a single ristretto shot in a 100 ml glass topped with warm, silky milk and minimal foam. Born in Australian specialty cafés, it delivers latte-like texture and sweetness in a compact, espresso-focused format. A café-culture insider's order.

name: Vienna Coffee
description: A Viennese classic: two strong espresso shots topped with a generous crown of hand-whipped cream, sometimes dusted with cocoa or cinnamon. Coffee is sipped through the cream rather than stirred in. A centrepiece of Vienna's legendary coffeehouse culture, or Kaffeehauskultur.

name: Cold Brew
description: Coarsely ground coffee steeped in cold or room-temperature water for 12–24 hours, then filtered into a smooth, low-acid concentrate. Served over ice, neat or diluted with water or milk. The slow cold extraction avoids releasing harsh acids and bitter oils, yielding a naturally sweet, chocolatey cup.

name: Iced Latte
description: Espresso shots poured over a tall glass of ice and filled with cold milk — the refrigerated counterpart to the hot caffè latte. Unlike a blended frappé, an iced latte is stirred, not blended, preserving the distinct espresso and milk layers that slowly mingle as you drink.

name: Iced Americano
description: Espresso shots pulled hot then poured over a large glass of ice and cold water. The flash-chill produces a slightly different extraction character than a hot Americano — lighter, brighter, and crisper with a noticeable acidity that makes it particularly refreshing on warm days.

name: Nitro Cold Brew
description: Cold brew coffee infused with nitrogen gas and served on tap through a stout-style faucet. The tiny nitrogen bubbles create a cascading, creamy head reminiscent of Guinness stout. Naturally sweet-tasting despite containing no sugar, with a uniquely velvety mouthfeel unlike any other coffee drink.

name: Iced Mocha
description: Espresso shaken or stirred with chocolate syrup, poured over ice, and topped with cold milk and whipped cream. The cooler temperature tames the bitterness of both the espresso and cocoa, turning this into a dessert-like treat that drinks almost like a chocolate coffee milkshake.

name: Iced Macchiato
description: Cold milk poured over ice first, then espresso shots slowly dropped through the milk to create a beautiful layered gradient from cream at the bottom to coffee at the top. Often sweetened with vanilla syrup. The espresso gradually sinks and mingles with the milk, changing flavour with every sip.

name: Caffè Shakerato
description: An Italian summertime classic: espresso and sugar syrup vigorously shaken with ice in a cocktail shaker, then strained into a chilled martini or coupe glass. The vigorous shaking creates a thick, frothy head and a refreshing chilled drink without the dilution of ice-filled glasses.

name: Greek Frappé
description: Instant coffee, sugar, and a small amount of water blended or vigorously shaken until it forms a thick, creamy foam, then poured over ice and topped with cold milk. Invented in Thessaloniki in 1957 by a Nescafé representative. The definitive summer drink of Greece for over six decades.

name: Cà Phê Sữa Đá
description: Vietnamese iced coffee brewed by dripping dark, intense robusta through a small phin filter directly into sweetened condensed milk, then stirred and poured over ice. The condensed milk adds deep caramelised sweetness that beautifully balances the bold, earthy robusta bitterness.

name: Mazagran
description: One of the oldest known cold coffee drinks, dating to 1840s French Algeria. Traditionally cold or iced black coffee served in a tall glass, sometimes sweetened and occasionally spiked with rum or lemon juice. Many coffee historians consider it the ancestor of all modern iced coffee drinks.

name: Pour Over
description: Hot water poured in a slow, controlled spiral over freshly ground coffee in a filter cone, allowing the coffee to bloom and extract gradually. Paper filtration produces an exceptionally clear, nuanced cup that highlights delicate floral, fruity, and tea-like notes often masked in espresso.

name: French Press
description: Coarsely ground coffee steeped in near-boiling water for 4 minutes, then a metal mesh plunger is pressed to separate the grounds. No paper filter means coffee oils and fine particles remain in the cup, producing a full-bodied, rich, slightly cloudy brew with exceptional depth and texture.

name: AeroPress
description: Invented in 2005 by Alan Adler, the AeroPress uses air pressure to force water through ground coffee in 1–3 minutes. Unmatched versatility — brew concentrated espresso-style shots or longer filter-style cups by adjusting grind, temperature, and steep time. A cult favourite in the specialty coffee world.

name: Chemex
description: Designed in 1941 by chemist Peter Schlumbohm, the Chemex is an hourglass-shaped glass decanter using a specially thick bonded paper filter that produces an exceptionally clean, bright, almost tea-like cup. Slower than most pour-over methods, it rewards patience with remarkable clarity and sweetness.

name: Moka Pot Coffee
description: Alfonso Bialetti's 1933 invention passes boiling water pressurised by steam through a basket of finely ground coffee into an upper chamber. Produces a strong, concentrated brew similar in intensity to espresso but without the crema, and with a slightly different pressure and temperature profile.

name: Drip Coffee
description: Hot water drips over ground coffee in a flat or conical paper filter, with gravity drawing the brew into a carafe below. The quintessential American morning ritual. Modern drip machines with precise temperature control can produce café-quality results rivalling manual pour-over methods.

name: Siphon Coffee
description: Also called a vacuum pot, the siphon brewer uses vapour pressure and vacuum to move water between two glass chambers separated by a filter. The resulting brew is astonishingly clear, bright, and complex — closer to sparkling tea than drip coffee. A theatrical method with a devoted following.

name: Clever Dripper
description: A full-immersion dripper with a valve: coffee steeps like a French press then drains through a paper filter when placed on a mug or carafe. Combines the body of immersion brewing with the clarity of paper filtration. Forgiving, consistent, and ideal for beginners exploring specialty coffee.

name: Turkish Coffee
description: Finely ground coffee — the finest grind of any brewing method — simmered with water and usually sugar in a small long-handled brass or copper pot called a cezve or ibrik. Poured unfiltered into a small cup; grounds settle at the bottom. Rich, thick, and aromatic, often spiced with cardamom.

name: Café de Olla
description: Brewed in an earthenware 'olla' (pot) with cinnamon and piloncillo (unrefined cane sugar), this traditional Mexican coffee is simmered slowly. The clay pot and spices infuse unique mineral and sweet notes into the brew. An ancient recipe that predates the espresso machine by centuries.

name: Café Cubano
description: A Cuban espresso brewed with sugar placed in the portafilter basket before extraction, creating a thick, sweet caramelised froth called 'espumita'. Extremely sweet and intensely caffeinated — served in tiny cups and traditionally shared among friends. A cornerstone of Cuban social life.

name: Café con Leche
description: Equal parts strong brewed coffee or espresso and hot scalded milk. A breakfast staple across Spain and Latin America, typically paired with toast or churros. The scalded milk — heated to near-boiling rather than steamed — adds a subtle cooked sweetness different from Italian milk-based drinks.

name: Dalgona Coffee
description: A 2020 global sensation from South Korea: instant coffee, sugar, and hot water whipped into a thick, glossy foam then spooned over cold or hot milk. Named after a Korean honeycomb toffee candy for its resemblance in flavour. Simple, photogenic, and surprisingly satisfying.

name: Bulletproof Coffee
description: Strong brewed coffee blended with grass-fed unsalted butter and MCT (medium-chain triglyceride) oil until smoothly emulsified into a latte-like drink. Popularised by Dave Asprey in 2011 and embraced by the keto and biohacking communities as a high-fat, zero-carb breakfast replacement.

name: Café Bombón
description: A visually striking Spanish specialty: sweetened condensed milk in the bottom of a small glass, then strong espresso carefully layered on top to create a dramatic two-tone gradient before stirring. Similar to Vietnamese iced coffee but served hot. A sweet, indulgent treat from Valencia.

name: Raf Coffee
description: A Russian specialty invented in Moscow in the 1990s: espresso, cream, and vanilla sugar are combined and steamed together — not separately as in a latte. The result is an incredibly smooth, almost dessert-like drink with uniform sweet creaminess throughout. Named after the customer who requested it.

name: Espresso Romano
description: A single espresso shot served with a thin slice of lemon on the side or rubbed around the cup rim. Despite the name, this drink is largely unknown in Rome — it was popularised in American Italian-inspired cafés. The lemon's citrus oils brighten the espresso and cut elegantly through its bitterness.

name: Malaysian White Coffee
description: Coffee beans roasted at roughly half the temperature of a typical light roast — often with palm oil margarine — producing a very pale, nutty brew with much higher natural sweetness and dramatically lower bitterness than conventionally roasted coffee. A signature of the Ipoh region's café culture.

name: Kopi Tubruk
description: The traditional Javanese and Balinese method: coarse coffee grounds boiled together with sugar and water in a cup, then left to settle before drinking — no filter, no press, just patience. Similar in approach to Turkish coffee but coarser and more rustic in character.

name: Caramel Macchiato
description: A popular coffeehouse creation: vanilla syrup and steamed milk in a cup, then marked with espresso shots poured through the foam, finished with a crosshatch drizzle of caramel sauce. Sipping through the caramel and foam reveals espresso, then sweet vanilla milk below. A layered experience.

name: Vanilla Latte
description: A caffè latte enhanced with vanilla syrup — one of the most universally beloved flavoured coffees. The vanilla's warm, sweet aroma complements espresso's roasty notes without overpowering them. Available hot, iced, or blended, making it the quintessential everyday flavoured coffee.

name: Hazelnut Latte
description: A latte flavoured with hazelnut syrup, producing a nutty, sweet beverage reminiscent of liquid Nutella. The hazelnut's natural affinity for dark-roasted coffee makes this pairing particularly harmonious. A perennial autumn favourite that shines with medium-dark or dark espresso roasts.

name: Oat Milk Cortado
description: A contemporary plant-based take on the cortado: espresso cut with an equal part of steamed oat milk. Oat milk's natural beta-glucan content gives it a surprisingly creamy texture and a subtle porridge sweetness that complements medium and dark espresso roasts beautifully. Dairy-free without compromise.

name: Coconut Latte
description: Espresso paired with steamed coconut milk or coconut syrup. Coconut milk's tropical sweetness and gentle richness create a lighter, exotic alternative to dairy lattes. Particularly popular in tropical countries and Australian beach-side cafés served with light-roast single-origin beans.

name: Pumpkin Spice Latte
description: A seasonal American coffeehouse icon launched by Starbucks in 2003: espresso with steamed milk flavoured with pumpkin puree, cinnamon, nutmeg, clove, and ginger, topped with whipped cream and a dusting of pumpkin pie spice. Has become a global symbol of the autumn season.

name: Irish Coffee
description: Invented in 1943 at Foynes Airport in County Limerick, Ireland: hot filter coffee sweetened with brown sugar, warmed Irish whiskey added, then topped with lightly whipped cream that floats on the surface. Coffee is drunk through the cool cream. A sophisticated, warming cold-weather drink.

name: Café au Lait
description: French for 'coffee with milk' — strong brewed coffee (traditionally from a French press or drip) combined with an equal part of hot steamed or scalded milk. Unlike a latte which uses espresso, café au lait is built on filter coffee, giving it a lighter, more rounded character. A Parisian breakfast ritual.

name: Gibraltar
description: A 90 ml drink popularised at Blue Bottle Coffee in San Francisco: a double ristretto in a 4.5 oz 'Gibraltar' glass topped with steamed whole milk. More concentrated than a cortado, slightly smaller than a flat white. The glass allows you to see the beautiful layers of espresso and milk merging.

name: Lungo Freddo
description: A chilled Italian coffee: a lungo extracted hot is immediately poured over ice in a short glass. The contrast between the hot, bitter lungo and cold ice creates a slightly different, more intense flavour than a regular iced Americano. A refreshing bridge between espresso culture and iced coffee.

name: Einspänner
description: A Viennese coffeehouse staple: a double espresso or strong black coffee served in a tall glass, topped with a generous amount of unsweetened whipped cream. Named after the one-horse carriages whose drivers would hold their drinks in one hand while steering with the other. Drunk through the cool cream.

name: Cascara Latte
description: Cascara (Spanish for 'husk') is a tea-like drink brewed from the dried husks of coffee cherries rather than the beans. Steeped in hot water it produces a fruity, hibiscus-like liquid with mild caffeine. Combined with steamed milk it becomes an exotic, light-bodied latte that surprises and delights first-timers.

name: Magic
description: A Melbourne, Australia specialty: two ristretto shots in a 150 ml cup topped with silky steamed whole milk — smaller and stronger than a flat white but with the same microfoam technique. It has never appeared on a mainstream coffee chain menu; ordering one signals that you know your specialty coffee.
